interpretation_summary: The replication executed exactly the preregistered Welch independent-samples t-test comparing summed intimacy scores between an attention and a no-attention group. Code ran in a clean Docker image and produced a positive mean difference (Mdiff = 2.01) but it was small (Cohen’s d = 0.34) and statistically non-significant (t = 1.10, p = .277). The original paper reported a much larger and highly significant effect (t = 5.91, d = 2.20). Hence, although the direction of the effect matched the original finding, the magnitude and significance did not replicate under the preregistered success criterion (p < .05 in the predicted direction). Overall, the replication does not support the focal claim given the available sample.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: The script created the reverse-coded intimacy scale, applied listwise deletion, and ran the preregistered Welch t-test with effect-size computation exactly as planned.

deviations.issue_description: Replication used a single new online sample (N = 41) rather than pooling several lab studies as in the original (~526).
deviations.impact: Moderate: Lower power likely contributed to non-significant result but does not affect analytic validity.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Participants who receive interpersonal attention will report higher summed intimacy scores than those who do not receive attention.
results_comparison.original_results: Attention condition M = 4.58 (SD = 0.82) vs No-attention M = 2.82 (SD = 0.79); t(27) = 5.91, p < .001; Cohen’s d = 2.20 (large positive effect).
results_comparison.replication_results: Attention condition M = 22.86 (SD = 6.04) vs No-attention M = 20.85 (SD = 5.61); Welch t(≈38) = 1.10, p = .277; Cohen’s d = 0.34 (small positive effect).
results_comparison.overall_answer: No – the replication produced a positive but non-significant effect, failing the preregistered criterion of p < .05 in the predicted direction.

replication_report: Replication unsuccessful: Interpersonal attention increased intimacy only slightly (d = 0.34) and not significantly (p = .277), diverging from the large, significant effect reported originally.

failure_handling.suggestions: Collect a larger sample to increase power; consider combining multiple replications or meta-analysis.
Ensure equivalence in context (lab vs online) and item scaling (original 7-point vs summed 6–42) to rule out measurement artifacts.

notes: With only 41 participants the study had ~25% power to detect the original d = 2.20 effect under conservative variance assumptions, but <10% power for the smaller observed effect. Future work should include larger preregistered samples and report item-level analyses or Bayesian evidence for absence of effect.
